Another interesting approach to carbon modification, the creation of a core-shell-structured carbon composite, was first applied by Kuribayashi et al. [129] More recently, other groups have also reported on the improved electrochemical performance of such composites. [130] The preparation is based on mixing the carbon precursors with graphite or graphitized carbon and heating the slurry mixtures at above \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). In Ref. 131 carbon-coated natural graphite was prepared by thermal vapor decomposition of natural graphite. [7] Li NMR spectra of the fully lithiated carbon-coated natural graphite show that there are two types of storage sites for lithium insertion: the graphite core part for lithium intercalation and the soft-carbon-type shell part for lithium storage. This material shows superior electrochemical performance as an anode for lithium-ion cells in both EC- and PC-based electrolytes. The irreversible capacity is inversely proportional to the amount of carbon coating.

It has been found that an increase in surface-oxygen content of a disordered carbon anode resulted in stable SEI formation. Using this property, a thin carbon coating on graphite can be implemented for improving capacity retention.[9]
Artificial SEI

The goals of artificial SEI formation,[132-135] are to improve the mechanical and thermal stability of the SEI, to reduce the irreversible capacity by preventing electrochemical decomposition of the electrolyte, increase reversible capacity and enable discharge and charge at higher rates.

A commonly adopted approach is to precondition the surfaces of active materials with coatings. Surface coating can change the reaction chemistry of SEI formation during battery operation, resulting in various SEI compositions or structures. The coating becomes part of the SEI; therefore, the coating has sometimes been referred to as an artificial SEI. The majority of artificial-SEI research has used single-component inorganic oxide or polymer coatings, such as \(\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}\), \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\), \(\mathrm{ZnO}\), \(\mathrm{SiO}_2\), polyelectrolytes and conducting polymers. Polymeric coating requires considerably low processing temperatures (typically \(< 200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\)) and, when water is used as a solvent, is an environmentally friendly alternative to the oxide coating.[133]

Peled et al.\(^{132}\) presented the concept of artificial SEI and studied two polyelectrolyte candidates for the formation of an artificial SEI: poly (ethylene-co-acrylic acid) (PEAA) and sodium carboxymethylcellulose (NaCMC). NaCMC has the advantage of thermal stability, smaller equivalent weight and lower resistance. Continuous, homogeneous films of the artificial SEI were formed on anode surface by electropainting on a graphite anode and vacuum-insertion into a nano tin-alloy anode. The artificial SEI stabilizes the structure of the anode on cycling. As a result, the modified tin anodes had five times the cycle life (where the end of cycle life is defined as the point at which the reversible capacity reaches \(50\%\) of its initial value) and less than half of the irreversible capacity of the pristine tin anode.

Winter et al.13 presented a new concept for forming a polymeric artificial SEI based on rational design of a multifunctional polymerblend coating to achieve favorable electrode/artificial SEI/electrolyte interfacial properties. Self-assembly between polymeric molecules as well as between polymers and the active material surface is enabled through interactions of specifically designed functional groups. The artificial SEI developed has led to considerable advance in specific capacity, rate performance, and cycling stability of natural graphite (NG), artificial graphite (AG), and Si-graphite (SG) composite containing silicon nanoparticles (NPs) on graphite-surface anodes. The most dominant effect was the substantial boost in delithiation performance, which corresponds to the discharging rate of an anode in full-cell operation. For the NG electrode, the specific delithiation capacity remained unchanged when the current was increased 50-fold, from 0.1 to \(5\mathrm{C}\) rate. The coated AG electrode exhibited \(80\%\) of full capacity at \(25\mathrm{C}\) rate, which is twice that of the bare AG electrode. The artificial SEI coating increased the delithiation rates of both the graphite and the silicon components of the SG electrode and increased
the specific capacity by more than a factor of six than that without a coating.\(^{133}\)

The design of the multi-functional polymeric SEI is based on the concept of introducing an interphase between the electrolyte and graphite which, as an alternative to the natural SEI, can accelerate the ion-transfer rate. [133] A polyether, polyethylene glycol tert-octylphenyl ether (PEGPE; \(\mathrm{C_{14}H_{22}O}\) \((\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{O})_\mathrm{n}\), \(n = 9 - 10\) ) was used as one component of the proposed artificial SEI. The lone pair electrons associated with the oxygen of ether groups can coordinate with \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) ions, in a manner similar to the lithium-ion-conduction mechanism of polyethylene oxide-based solid electrolytes. It may provide more transport pathways than does a grained nanocomposite SEI. The other half of the PEGPE molecule contains an alkyl terminal attached to an aromatic ring. The aromatic ring enables \(\pi-\pi\) attractive interaction with the graphite surface to achieve strong adhesion. This feature distinguishes PEGPE from PEO and affords PEGPE with superior compatibility with the graphite surface. PEGPE is a liquid at room temperature and so a solid polymer polyallyl amine was added to provide the necessary coating integrity and mechanical strength. Polyallyl amine was designed to anchor PEGPE molecules through strong hydrogen-bridge bonding between \(\mathrm{NH}_{2}\) in polyallyl amine and O in PEGPE. The lone pair electrons in the nitrogen atoms of the amine groups were used to coordinate \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) ions. The thickness of the resulting artificial SEI layer was about \(8 - 10\mathrm{nm}\) and it was thermally stable, according to TGA, up to \(200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). [133]

Liang et al. [134] offered a different approach to the design of artificial SEI films for high-capacity Li-ion battery silicon-alloy-based electrodes by using solid electrolyte materials. Lithium phosphorus oxynitride (LIPON) was chosen for the fabrication of the artificial SEI because of its reasonable ionic conductivity \((2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~S.cm}^{-1})\) and good electrochemical stability at low potential. When the LIPON artificial SEI is thicker than \(50\mathrm{nm}\), the coulombic efficiency increases significantly to \(99 + \%\) and according to the differential capacity-voltage profile, no obvious reduction peak of the electrolyte can be seen.

Guo et al. [135] showed that an artificial \(50~\mathrm{nm}\) \(\mathrm{Li}_3\mathrm{PO}_4\) SEI layer with high Young's modulus can retard lithium-dendrite growth and reduce the side reaction between lithium metal and the organic electrolyte. The native film of lithium metal was replaced by the stable and uniform \(\mathrm{Li}_3\mathrm{PO}_4\) SEI layer via an in-situ treatment process. The resulting \(\mathrm{Li}_3\mathrm{PO}_4\) SEI layer exhibits high chemical stability during the lithium deposition/dissolution process without a breakage and repair mechanism. Thus, it effectively retards lithium-dendrite growth and reduces the corrosion of bulk lithium after 200 cycles in a \(\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{LiFePO}_4\) battery.
Future Systems

SEI on sodium in sodium-ion and sodium-air batteries. In contrast to the lithium-ion cell, the rechargeable lithium-air cell has a metallic-lithium anode. In order to develop a practical, safe, smaller and lower-cost lithium-air and sodium-air rechargeable batteries it is necessary to eliminate the formation of dendritic deposits (on charge), and to increase the power capability of the batteries. With this goal in mind, a novel concept was suggested in,[136,137] namely to replace the metallic-lithium anode by liquid sodium and to operate the sodium-oxygen cell above the sodium melting point \((97.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\). In order to demonstrate that molten sodium can be cycled, the researchers ran deposition-dissolution tests of sodium on aluminum at \(105^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) with the use of a coin cell. The electrolyte consisted of NaTf \(\mathrm{(NaCF_3SO_3)}\) \(\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}\), PEO (MW of \(5\times 10^{6}\)) and methyl methanesulfonate as an SEI precursor.

The typical thickness of the anode SEI in lithium and lithium-ion batteries is in the range of \(10 - 100\mathrm{nm}\). The resistances of both SEIs, the SEI on the sodium-metal counter electrode and the SEI on the deposited sodium metal on the working electrode, are similar to those measured for the lithium anode in an electrolyte consisting of NaTf, \(\mathrm{Al_2O_3}\), methyl methanesulfonate and PEO (MW of \(5\times 10^{6}\)). The thickness of the sodium SEI is of the order of
Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 164 (7) A1703-A1719 (2017)
\(10\mathrm{nm}\) for a fresh electrode, close to that of lithium metal in nonaqueous battery electrolytes.

One of the main conclusions of the research was that in order to create a protective SEI on alkali-metal anodes, it is essential that the equivalent volumes of the SEI materials be greater than that of the metal anode. Only in this way does the SEI completely cover the anode surface and stop corrosion. If not, the anode will continue to corrode. For example, the equivalent volumes of \(\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}\), \(\mathrm{NaF}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_2O}\) are 20.87, 16 and \(13.7~\mathrm{mL}\), respectively, all lower than that of sodium \((23.7~\mathrm{mL})\). Thus, these cannot serve as good SEI-building materials. On the other hand, the equivalent volumes of several sodium oxosulfur compounds including \(\mathrm{Na_2S_2O_4}\), \(\mathrm{Na_2S_2O_3}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}\) are 39.7, 47.4 and \(26.7\mathrm{mL}\), respectively, which makes them suitable candidates for use as sodium-SEI-building materials. Thus, the common ethylene carbonate/ diethyl carbonate (EC/DEC) \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) electrolyte does not contain good sodium-SEI precursors and some sulfur-containing materials must be added. Since the solid electrolyte interphase, formed on the anode during battery operation is the most vulnerable factor in the safety and cycle life of the sodium-air battery, the formation of a proper SEI is a critical process.[136,137]

A comparative study of the electrode/electrolyte interphase was carried out by A. Ponrouch et al.\(^{138}\) for lithium- and sodium-metal anodes in electrolytes consisting of \(1\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) in \(\mathrm{EC}_{0.5}:\mathrm{DMC}_{0.5}\) (LP30) and \(1\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\) in both \(\mathrm{EC}_{0.5}:\mathrm{DMC}_{0.5}\) and \(\mathrm{EC}_{0.45}\mathrm{PC}_{0.45}\mathrm{DMC}_{0.1}\) at room temperature. Symmetric Li/Li cells exhibited low polarization and smooth charge/discharge curves with current densities of 0.1 and \(1\mathrm{mA/cm^2}\). By contrast, large overpotentials were observed even at \(0.1\mathrm{mA/cm^2}\) for Na/Na cells. The differences were related to an enhanced interfacial resistance \((\mathrm{Rct} + \mathrm{R_{SEI}})\) of sodium-metal SEI, as deduced from impedance measurements.\(^{138}\) The surface morphology of the Li- and Na-metal electrodes was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with pristine Li and Na electrodes and after immersion in the electrolytes at OCV for 24 hours, by which time a thin and smooth SEI (no crack and no protruding particles) was observed for the Li-metal electrode, while the surface of the Na-metal electrode appeared to be more rough. Impedance measurements were performed before and after CV in order to obtain more information about the potential resistivity differences between the systems studied. In all cases, a depressed semicircle appears after cycling, indicative of the formation of an SEI. However, the charge-transfer resistance associated with these semicircles is significantly higher in the case of the Na-anode cell than for the Li-anode cell and indicate a significantly less resistive SEI in the latter. In the sodium-based system, including the use of a hard carbon electrode, the initial impedance plot shows a large high-frequency loop, which extends over more than 30 \(\Omega\). This grows with time and reaches more than \(50\Omega\). This growth rate is faster than that of the semicircle. This indicates that the interface between hard carbon and the electrolyte becomes more resistive with time, and its nature is more complex. Since this effect of increase in resistance of the interface between hard carbon and electrolyte was not observed in symmetrical cells or in a three-electrode cell with Li and LP30, it can be attributed to the presence of sodium metal in the cell. The authors suggest that the electrolyte could decompose chemically on sodium, and the decomposition products could form a deposit on the carbon electrode. The presence of sodium metal in the electrolyte influences the surface chemistry of the carbon electrode at OCV. The authors claim that the salts constituting the SEI formed on the sodium-metal electrode are less stable than those on lithium-metal electrode.\(^{138}\)

SEI formation and functionality, as well as cell self-discharge, were studied for the sodium-ion system with comparative experiments on the equivalent lithium-ion system.[139] The impact of SEI dissolution was studied by conducting a set of experiments on carbonaceous anodes. The potential of the carbon electrode was monitored in situ by sampling of the cell voltage. When the voltage increases during a pause time, it means that the electrode is being drained of its alkali ions and this, in turn, means that the SEI is not passivating the electrode sufficiently. The charge capacity lost as a consequence of the 100-hour pause was \(80\%\) of the sodium-cell charge capacity but only \(20\%\) of

the lithium-cell charge capacity. The results show that the SEI layer in sodium-ion cells, containing \(1\mathrm{MNaPF_6}\) dissolved in EC/DEC (1:1) electrolyte, is inferior to that of its lithium-ion counterpart, containing \(1\mathrm{M}\mathrm{LiPF}_6\) dissolved in EC/DEC (1:1) electrolyte, with regard to self-discharge. Sodium cells show a loss in capacity at a dramatic rate as compared to the lithium cells when they are stored under OCV conditions at sodiated and lithiated states, respectively. Furthermore, it was also shown that the self-discharge behavior can be altered and improved by additives even though in this case, for example, FEC possibly changes the problem from high self-discharge to high polarization.[139]

Other researchers[139] often attributed the self-discharge phenomenon to redox shuttles, SEI dissolution, or cracking/delamination and to solvent permeability through the SEI. To determine whether the sodium SEI problems result from the solubility of the SEI component or permeability of the solvent, synchrotron-based hard X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HAXPES) measurements were performed on carbon electrodes that had been left to rest after CV for different lengths of time. Two different photon energies were used and the electrolyte was \(1\mathrm{MNaPF_6}\) dissolved in EC/DEC (1:1). According to the analysis by HAXPES,[139] the thickness of the SEI does not increase during pauses but instead decreases. These results support the idea that the solubility of the SEI rather than permeation of solvent through it is the primary cause of self-discharge. The reason for the increased solubility of sodium might be the lower tendency of sodium organometallic compounds to polymerize because of lower charge density. This line of argument can also explain why only a few of the film-forming additives from LIBs are effective in SIBs.[139]

The structural change and passivation of hard carbon and the reversible lithium and sodium intercalation were studied at room temperature and \(60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The electrolyte consisted of propylene carbonate (PC) and 1 M \(\mathrm{LiClO_4}\) or \(\mathrm{NaClO_4}\). XPS results indicated that the hard carbon in both electrolytes is covered by decomposition products of the electrolytes after electrochemical tests with similar depth distribution of chemical species. The difference in the surface-film components is evidenced by TOF-SIMS. In the sodium cell, small numbers of fragment peaks are observed, and their intensities are markedly higher than those of the lithium cell. The fragments were mainly assigned to inorganic components such as \(\mathrm{Na_2O^+}\), \(\mathrm{Na_2OH^+}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_2F^+}\). On the other hand, most of the fragments observed in the lithium cell were assigned to organic species, including \(\mathrm{C_4H_3^+}\), \(\mathrm{C_2H_5O_3^+}\) and \(\mathrm{C_2H_2O_5Li_3^+}\). These results prove that the surface film in the lithium cell is composed mainly of organic compounds, while that in the sodium cell is composed of inorganic compounds and this enables the reversible sodium intercalation of the hard carbon.[140]

SEI on calcium and magnesium anodes (t_close to 1).—As opposed to alkali-metal anodes, which have SEI with \( t_+ \) close to unity, in many cases, alkaline-earth metals (AEM), like calcium, are covered by an SEI that consists of \( \mathrm{CaF}_2 \) or \( \mathrm{CaCl}_2 \) which are anionic conductors (t is close to 1). In calcium and magnesium batteries the charging step requires the deposition of the alkali metal at high faradaic efficiency. In thionyl chloride electrolyte calcium behaves like an "electrochemical diode", it can be discharge at high rates but cannot charge even at very high voltage (exceeding \( 40\mathrm{V} \)).[141] This is explained by the fact that the \( \mathrm{CaCl}_2 \) based SEI is an anionic conductor (t. close to unity). Calcium and other alkaline-earth metals may be deposited in cases where the SEI is very fresh, disordered and contains many defects, especially at elevated temperatures. In these cases, as long as the SEI is fresh and disordered, the alkaline-earth metal may be deposited, at low faradaic efficiency, in parallel to the reduction of the electrolyte to form the SEI. However, under OCV storage of the freshly deposited AEM, the SEI becomes more ordered with fewer defects and thicker and it will be impossible to deposit it. There will be a need for a prior step of calcium dissolution, before the deposition step, in order to reduce the thickness of the SEI and to form defects in its structure. Working at elevated temperature as describe by Ref. 142 may create such conditions of a thin SEI with defects which may allow calcium deposition in parallel with electrolyte reduction. Aurbach et al. suggested

that the magnesium battery, with an electrolyte based on alkyl magnesium and ethers, is probably an SEI-free system in which magnesium can be deposited.\(^{143}\) However, under long cycling or extended OCV storage, it is expected that a passivating layer will be formed on the anode which will make it difficult to deposit magnesium.\(^{142}\)

The feasibility of reversible calcium electrodeposition in carbonate-based electrolytes, containing salts with known stable anions, has been assessed through cyclic voltammetry in a cell consisting of a stainless-steel working electrode and a calcium-metal counter-reference electrode. No redox processes in the potential window investigated (from \(-1.5\) to \(2\mathrm{V}\) versus \(\mathrm{Ca^{2 + } / Ca}\) passivated) could be detected at room temperature. Nonetheless, at higher temperatures \((50 - 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\) and for electrolytes containing \(\mathrm{Ca(ClO_4)_2}\) and \(\mathrm{Ca(BF_4)_2}\), a redox process is observed, with an intensity dependent on salt concentration and increasing with temperature. However, on examination of cyclic voltammetry of calcium plating and stripping (Figure 3 in Ref. 142), it appears that the charge capacity is significantly greater than the discharge capacity, which indicates very low current efficiency. The thickest calcium deposit was observed for \(\mathrm{Ca(BF_4)_2}\) at \(75^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The corresponding synchrotron X-ray diffraction pattern exhibits reflections corresponding to calcium metal and \(\mathrm{CaF}_2\) as major phases. These results confirm the fact that the deposit contains calcium metal and that the observed redox process does indeed correspond to reversible calcium plating/stripping. \(\mathrm{CaF}_2\) is certainly derived from electrolyte decomposition and most likely part of the SEI, in agreement with similar studies conducted on Li-ion batteries reporting the presence of LiF in passivation layers formed in \(\mathrm{LiBF_4}\)-containing electrolytes. The fact that deposits grown at \(100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) exhibit larger amounts of calcium than those grown at \(75^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for the same deposition time, indicates that the temperature may enhance the kinetics of electroplating while having a limited effect on the electrolyte decomposition to form the SEI.[144] It was found that cation diffusivities in \(\mathrm{CaF}_2\) are extremely low and this tends to rule out cation conduction.[145-147] This means that \(\mathrm{CaF}_2\) cannot serve as a good SEI material under normal conditions, as previously explained.

SEI on silicon anodes.-The processes taking place in the first intercalation of lithium into an alloy anode in a lithium-ion battery assembled in the discharged state are expected to be very similar to those in carbon anodes. Most of the intercalation of lithium into the alloy takes place after reduction of the electrolytes and building of the SEI. A minor amount of lithium can be intercalated at high voltage (above \(1\mathrm{V}\) on the lithium scale) prior to, or in parallel with SEI formation. This SEI buildup consumes part of the cathode capacity and is called an "irreversible" capacity \((Q_{\mathrm{ir}})\) loss (or ICL). Because of the dependence of \(\mathrm{i_o}\) for the reduction of an electrolyte component, on the catalytic nature of the alloy surface, the chemical composition and morphology of the SEI may vary from alloy to alloy as well from one electrolyte to another. The large reversible volume changes in some lithium alloys during charge/discharge cycles may result in shorter cycle life and lower faradaic efficiency as a result of the formation of cracks in both the alloy and in the SEI. Therefore, in this case, the flexibility of the SEI is of great importance. In the case of the silicon anodes, this "breathing" phenomenon of the anode particles is very significant and causes cracks, exposing the bare silicon sur

face to the electrolyte, followed by the creation of a fresh SEI, thus consuming electrolyte and battery capacity, leading to the increase in the impedance of the battery. Si nanostructures have the advantage of a shorter diffusion distance for lithium species, which can improve the power performance of the battery. It has been shown that the high surface-to-volume ratio of Si nanoparticles helps to better withstand stress, and substantially limit the cracking extent.[148] The existence of a strong particle-size-dependent fracture behavior of Si nanoparticles during the first lithiation cycle was shown experimentally,[149] that is, there exists a critical particle size of \(\sim 150\mathrm{nm}\), below which cracking does not occur, and above which surface cracking and particle fracture takes place.

The lithiation de-lithiation process of nano Si particles is shown schematically in Figure 6. During the first lithiation the volume of the Si nano particle expands and a compact SEI forms around it. At the end of the first discharge the Si nano particle shrinks back to its original (or close to) size, thus its surface decreases. As a result, the extra SEI materials are pushed out to form a secondary porous layer whose pores are filled with electrolyte. The thickness of this porous layer grows and its porosity decreases (increasing the Labyrinth factor) with cycle number leading to limitation of the transport of ions in the electrolyte thus to loss of capacity and power.

Cui et al. [150] have characterized the surface composition and morphology of the SEI formed on the silicon nano wires (SiNWs) in 1 M \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) EC: DEC electrolyte using XPS and SEM. The authors [150] have shown that high capacities can be obtained and maintained for several cycles and that irreversible capacity loss is not caused by lost or disconnected material, but rather is due to side reactions and SEI formation.

In the first charge to \(0.5\mathrm{V}\), the SEI layer is composed almost equally of hydrocarbons, PEO-type oligomers, LiF, \(\mathrm{Li_xPF_y}\) and \(\mathrm{Li_xPF_yO_z}\) products, with smaller amounts of lithium alkyl carbonates and ethers. On the other hand, in the first charge to \(0.1\mathrm{V}\), the SEI surface consisted predominately

of hydrocarbons and PEO-oligomers, indicating that more electrolyte was being reduced. \(\mathrm{Li_2CO_3}\) was also observed but no Li alkyl carbonates or ethers were observed, suggesting that these less stable products were transformed into \(\mathrm{Li_2CO_3}\). The amount of LiF and \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) was lower than that at \(0.5\mathrm{V}\).

Finally, at the full charge to \(10\mathrm{mV}\), the SEI surface consisted mainly of \(\mathrm{Li_2CO_3}\) and LiF. The \(\mathrm{Li_2CO_3}\) is formed as a product of electrochemical reduction of the electrolyte, but the LiF is formed as a decomposition product rather than an electrochemical product. The morphology of the SEI was found to be voltage-dependent, with a thick layer consisting of large particles forming at low potentials, which partially dissolves and forms cracks as the potential is increased in the discharge. Improved cycling performance was observed when the voltage cutoffs were tuned to avoid large changes in SEI morphology, suggesting that the cycle life in SiNWs may rely a great deal on the SEI. This may be due to the enhanced reactivity of SiNWs as a result of the large surface area, as well as the composition of SEI products formed on the surface of the nanowire.[150]
A novel approach, based on three-dimensional (3D) growth of highly dense, mostly amorphous SiNWs on carbon-fiber-based open-structure conductive networks, was recently published. The
Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 164 (7) A1703-A1719 (2017)

electrolyte was \(1\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) in EC/DEC (1:1) with \(2\%\) VC, to which fluoroethylenecarbonate (FEC) (15% by volume, Solvay-Fluor) was added.[151] Several degradation mechanisms plausibly involved in the charge/discharge process of SiNW-based anodes were considered. It was concluded that the major reason for the capacity loss in the tested cells is an increase in cell impedance and not particle-to-particle and particle-to-current collector contact loss, disintegration or pulverization of the SiNWs. After 200 cycles, \(80\%\) of the SiNWs maintain their low rate capacity. The continuous SEI growth is caused by the reduction of the electrolyte salt and solvents leading to precipitation of solids, and drying out of the cell. This leads to capacity loss at high rates and suppresses the high rate capability of the anode. In addition, it was found that \(\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{SEI}}\) of the lithiated anode is smaller than that of the delithiated anode. This is mainly caused by the formation of a fresh, and thus lower-resistance SEI as a result of the expansion of the SiNWs and by the increase in their surface area. The low Qir, high efficiency and long cycle life of the SiNW anode are ascribed to two factors: the amorphous SiNW shell, and the nonparallel structure of the SiNWs leaving enough open space for electrolyte-reduction products and for the secondary porous SEI growth, without blocking the electrolyte path to the bulk of the anode.

The total SEI thickness, (including both the compact and the second porous layer) that was measured with the use of SEM, found to increase from \(22\mathrm{nm}\) after the first cycle to \(72\mathrm{nm}\) after the \(11^{\mathrm{th}}\) cycles.[151] In addition, it was found that all measured SEI parameters for the 3D SiNW anode - \(\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{SEI}}\), \(\rho_{\mathrm{SEI}}\) and SEI thickness - grow with cycle number. However, the growth of the secondary porous SEI was much higher than the growth of the compact SEI (measured by EIS). These observations point to the fact that the insufficiently stable SEI that leads to the intensive growth of the secondary porous SEI is the major factor in the observed capacity loss.[151]

P. Grey et al.\(^{152}\) investigated the SEI growth on nano silicon-carbon and carboxy methyl cellulose (CMC) composite electrode, which was formed from the reduction of a standard 1M \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) in EC/DMC electrolyte and directly measured and quantified with the use of \(^{7}\mathrm{Li}\), \(^{19}\mathrm{F}\), and \(^{13}\mathrm{C}\) ssNMR. In the first stage, the capacity loss is correlated with a growth of the SEI and increasing electrode tortuosity. The increased electrode density detected by FIB/SEM was ascribed to a combination of SEI formation on the silicon particles, particle agglomeration and SEI exfoliation from particle surfaces filling the pores of the electrode. In the second stage, the decrease in lithiation capacity was attributed to kinetics. The results support a model in which lithium-ion diffusion is severely limited by the increased electrode tortuosity throughout the bulk of the electrode.

L. Giebeler et al. studied the possible degradation mechanisms of silicon nanoparticles attached to a porous carbon-scaffold anode as a function of the electrolyte composition.[153] A silicon-carbon anode was tested vs. a lithium-metal counter electrode in 1M \(\mathrm{LiPF_6}\) in EC/DMC (1:1) (LP30 Selectilyte, BASF) without additives. Two major degradation mechanisms occur and both are attributed to the rapidly and continuously growing SEI: loss of electrical contact of individual silicon particles to the carbon scaffold and loss of ionic contact as a result of high film resistance which suppresses \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) migration. After the addition of fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC), the film resistance decreases dramatically and the SEI growth rate is considerably lower. However, the growth of the SEI cannot be completely suppressed and eventually a thick SEI is formed, causing a loss of electrical contact between individual silicon particles. With the addition of VC, the growth of the SEI is nearly completely suppressed and the loss of electrical and ionic contact is prevented. However, the dense surface film results in low \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) conductivity, hindering the use of high currents. The authors concluded that the VC additive for nano-silicon anodes clearly outperforms the FEC additive in terms of lifetime and efficiency owing to the formation of a very flexible surface film which survives the large volume changes. However, the surface film shows high film resistance to \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) migration as a rate-determining step, likely due to the absence of defects in the film structure, making VC unfavorable for high-power applications. In contrast, the FEC-derived SEI shows less flexibility and reversibility compared to VC, but offers re

markable conductivity for \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) ions, which are essential requirements for high power applications. The authors suggest that the polymeric properties of both, VC and FEC-derived SEIs are equal; however, in the case of FEC additional LiF nanocrystals are embedded within the surface film. These crystallites may cause defects in the homogeneous structure of the SEI, enabling enhanced \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\)-ion conductivity, but at the expense of reversibility and cycle life. The compact inorganic nanocrystals decrease the flexibility of the surface film, which results in cracks during the volume changes of the silicon particles, which eventually causes a loss of electrical contact to the carbon. In the absence of any electrolyte additive, both loss of electrical and ionic contact are responsible for rapid degradation.

Meng et al.\(^{154}\) studied the manner in which FEC affects the evolution of SEI in terms of composition and morphology throughout electrochemical cycling in silicon nano-particle anodes. The authors concluded that the electrode, cycled in standard lithium-ion electrolyte with FEC additive, is covered with a uniform and stable SEI mainly at the first few cycles, while the SEI of the electrode cycled without additives immediately forms a porous and inhomogeneous SEI. The main reasons for better results are assigned to FEC decomposition products which eliminate the formation of a \(\mathrm{Li_xSiO_y}\) layer which leads to the immediate formation of a homogeneous, dense SEI.

Jung et al. [155] showed that FEC can indeed improve the lifetime of silicon-carbon composite anodes but is continuously consumed during electrochemical cycling. FEC is capable of stabilizing cell performance only as long as it remains in the cell. Its total consumption causes a significant increase of the cell polarization leading to a rapid capacity drop. A new reductive decomposition mechanism for FEC-based electrolyte is proposed, which yields \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\), LiF, \(\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{O}\), \(\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}\), \(\mathrm{H}_{2}\) and a partially cross-linked polymer. The authors showed that FEC nearly suppresses the reduction of any other electrolyte component, which suggests that the rate of FEC reduction is greater than the rate of reduction of EC and linear carbonates. Furthermore, the number of charge/discharge cycles over which silicon anodes can be stabilized by FEC is directly proportional to the total moles of FEC per gram of anode, rather than the FEC concentration in the electrolyte. The experiments were carried out with anode material comprising silicon nano particles (\(\sim 100\mathrm{nm}\)), vapor-grown carbon fibers and lithium polyacrylate (LiPAA) binder at 40:40:20 ratio. The FEC was added to \(1\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{LiPF}_6\) EC:EMC 3:7 w/w electrolyte.

H. Song et al.\(^{156}\) compared the lithiation kinetics of nano and micro silicon anode particles. It was concluded that nanoparticle silicon benefits from smaller normalized resistance (Rp) and a thinner SEI layer compared to its micro-size counterpart. The results confirm the fact that the nano-sized anodes appear to be less dependent on the C-rate and show smaller values of Rp.

SEI on tin and tin oxide anodes. Nano-Sn, \(\mathrm{AlSi}_{0.1}\) and \(\mathrm{Li}_{4.4}\mathrm{Sn}\) powders were studied by EIS to determine the electrochemical kinetics and intrinsic resistance during initial lithium intercalation-extraction. It was shown that the SEI formed on the surfaces of the particles, together with their pulverization, are responsible for the high contact resistance. With the use of a high-speed-electroplating method (at currents above the limiting-current density), Peled and Ulus[157-160] were able to produce two nanosize tin-antimony alloys. In Refs. 157,160 two sets of composite anodes - \(\mathrm{Sn}_{65}\mathrm{Sb}_{18}\mathrm{Cu}_{17}\) and \(\mathrm{Sn}_{62}\mathrm{Sb}_{21}\mathrm{Cu}_{17}\) - were produced. The weight ratio between the alloy and the PVDF-graphite material was 70:30. Graphite composite was added in order to enhance the resistance of the fragile porous structure of the alloy to cycling effects (volume changes, particle-to-particle breakoff), and to sustain continuous electronic conductivity. The reversible capacity of the composite anode in a half-cell was \(495\mathrm{mAh / g}\) and the irreversible capacity was \(25\%\). The capacity loss until cycle 35 was \(0.48\%\) per cycle, which is comparable to the capacity loss \((0.51\%)\) of a similar alloy with no graphite. In a tin alloy/LiCoO\(_2\) cell, the anode reversible capacity was \(532\mathrm{mAh / g}\), the irreversible capacity was \(35\%\) and the capacity loss (until cycle 20) was \(0.9\%\) per cycle. The dQ/dV curves of charge/discharge showed broadening of peaks between 300-1400 mV; this may indicate slower kinetics resulting from thickening
Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 164 (7) A1703-A1719 (2017)

of the SEI. The SEI thickening in turn, is due to active material disintegration and exposure of the active material to the electrolyte. As the cycle number increases, the SEI thickens and the migration of ions between particles becomes sluggish. A composite anode material, which contained a tin-antimony-copper \((\mathrm{Sn}_{60}\mathrm{Sb}_{20}\mathrm{Cu}_{20})\) alloy of \(100\mathrm{nm}\) particle size, graphite flakes with a particle size of \(3 - 20\mathrm{mm}\) and PVDF as a binder was studied.[10] As in the previous case, the main degradation mechanism of the composite anode is related to particle-to-particle break-off, and to the disconnection of particles from the current collector. A secondary degradation mechanism results from the thickening of the SEI layer with cycle number and its subsequent cracking, and is influenced by the electrolyte composition. XPS analysis indicates that after 30 cycles, tin and tin compounds were not identified in the SEI,[10] even after 40 minutes of sputtering. This agrees with thickening of the SEI. The elemental depth profile of the electrode after six cycles, revealed that the concentration of Li decreased from 27 to 10 atomic \(\%\) , and the concentration of F decreased from 17 to \(9\%\) . The oxygen content during the sputtering process was almost constant. Therefore, the excess of lithium over fluorine was attributed to \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3\) and \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{O}\) . Only low concentrations of hydrocarbons and oxygen-containing polymeric compounds were detected in the SEI. In addition, the LiF content in the SEI increases with cycle number from 20 to \(48\%\) . Such SEI composition with high concentrations of inorganic salts and low content of polymeric compounds is expected to be followed by increased brittleness and possible break-off on cycling. It was concluded that that the degradation mechanism of the active material in tin-based alloys involves particle-to-particle separation, and the thickening and cracking of the SEI with increasing cycle number.[10]

V. Winkler et al.[161] investigated the chemical composition of the SEI formed in LiTFSI-based electrolytes using VC and FEC as performance-improving additives for nano-particulate \(\mathrm{SnO}_2\) film anodes. During the first discharging cycle of \(\mathrm{SnO}_2\) anodes, metallic tin and lithium oxide are formed and are responsible for Qir. Subsequent charging and discharging correlate to lithiation/delithiation reaction in a kind of alloying/dealloying of tin. The study showed the existence of a high surface reactivity of nanoparticulate \(\mathrm{SnO}_2\) electrodes leading to the formation of a significant amount of oxidized carbon surface species, mainly \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3\), already at the initial cycling. This irreversible consumption of lithium for SEI forming is the main reason for low coulombic efficiencies. The high surface reactivity of \(\mathrm{SnO}_2\) leads to preferred decomposition of the main electrolyte components, EC and DMC. This complicates the identification of VC-related SEI species via XPS. Despite this, the FEC additive could better influence the SEI composition, resulting in further improvement of the cycling properties as compared to those of the pure LiTFSI or LiTFSI+VC electrolytes.[161]

SEI on lithium anodes in lithium/sulfur batteries.—Capacity fading of the lithium/sulfur battery on cycling results from at least four processes: increase of SEI thickness and SEI resistance on the lithium anode, loss of cathode capacity (precipitation of sulfur species outside the cathode), agglomeration and thickening of core-shell sulfur species and increase in cell impedance as a result of reduction of the electrolyte. On discharge, two transport processes (diffusion and migration) contribute to the transport of polysulfide anions to the lithium anode. This leads to an increase in the concentration of polysulfides near the anode. On the other hand, during charge, the polysulfide concentration near the anode decreases. The reactions of polysulfides with lithium are complex; short ones are expected to cause precipitation of \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{S}\) and \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{S}_2\) as lithium-corrosion products, while long polysulfides may partially dissolve \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{S}\) and \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{S}_2\) particles from the SEI. Reaction of the anode with PSs and with the electrolyte may lead to the thickening of the primary and the secondary SEI. However, it was recently found that both \(\mathbb{R}_{\mathrm{SEI}}\) and SEI thickness change very little on cycling (Table I). [28,162]

Xiong et al. [163] used 1M \(\mathrm{LiN(CF_3SO_2)_2}\) (LiTFSI) in 1,3-dioxolane (DIOX) and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME, 1:1 v/v) mixed solvents in a symmetrical coin cell consisting of two lithium-metal electrodes, to study the SEI on the lithium anode in a lithium/sulfur battery. The authors found that the surface film on the lithium electrode stabilizes after a certain number of cycles. However, after 105 cycles, the resistance value increases, demonstrating the disruption of the surface film. The symmetrical cell cycled in the electrolyte solution with polysulfides results in a stable potential, which means that the system has a stable interface between the lithium electrode and the electrolyte solution. Furthermore, SEM images show that the decomposition of lithium salt on the lithium electrode can be prevented by the surface film formed with polysulfides, which is attributed to the special structure of the film. Based on the XPS spectra and depth profiling, the authors assumed that the surface film consists of two sub-layers - a top layer composed of the products of electrolyte-solution decomposition and a bottom layer of lithium sulfide. [163]

Zheng et al. [164] found that anode consumption accelerated by a corrosive polysulfide solution, critically determines the Li/S cell performance. The ionic liquid (IL) N-methyl-N-butylypyrrolidinium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide (Py14TFSI) has been employed to modify the properties of the SEI formed on the lithium-metal surface in Li/S batteries. It was found that the IL-enhanced formation of a passivating film on the lithium-anode surface exhibits very different morphology and chemical composition, effectively protecting lithium metal from continuous attack by soluble polysulfides. During storage, in contrast to the common lithium-ion electrolyte cells, in cells with the IL-containing \((50\%)\) IL electrolyte, the cell impedance is much more stable, despite a slight increase in the total resistance during the first few days. To modify the SEI on the Li-metal surface, IL Py14TFSI has been incorporated as a co-solvent into the conventional electrolyte. When \(75\%\) IL-containing electrolyte was used in the Li/S battery system, the electrochemical performance was significantly improved. The authors concluded that the ionic liquid facilitates the formation of a stable SEI of improved quality. This dense SEI effectively prevents the continuous penetration of soluble polysulfides to the surface of the anode, which slows its rapid corrosion and eliminates the increases in the impedance. [164] EIS measurements of a cycled lithium/sulfur cell reveals that, in some cases, \(\mathrm{R_{SEI}}\) almost does not change during the first 200 cycles. It was found that both the type of the cathode binder and the type of the polysulfide barrier layer affect the performance and the fading rate of the battery. An optimized combination of a binder and a barrier layer yield an initial capacity of \(1400\mathrm{mAh / g}\) (per sulfur mass), over \(99.5\%\) coulombic efficiency and close to 1000 cycles. Capacity fading results from the increase in both \(\mathrm{R_{SEI}}\) and \(\mathrm{R_{CT}}\) and the decrease in D during prolonged cycling. D decreases as a result of several processes, including partial blocking of the cathode surface, precipitation of electrolyte reduction products on the anode (as a secondary \(\mathrm{SEI}^{10}\)) and into the separator.
Cathodic SEI.—According to Novak et al.,<sup>165</sup> a protective film similar to SEI is also reported for cathode materials such as \(\mathrm{LiMn_2O_4}\),
Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 164 (7) A1703-A1719 (2017)

\(\mathrm{LiCoO_2}\), and \(\mathrm{LiNiO_2}\). It was shown (Aurbach<sup>166</sup>) that the cell impedance, of aged cells, originates mainly from the transition metal oxide cathode. This indicates the possibility of the formation of a surface film on the oxide.<sup>166</sup> As in the case of graphite anode materials, the surface film at the electrode/electrolyte interface of the oxides leads to an irreversible “charge loss”. Different parameters may have an impact on the formation and properties of this surface film, including; The type of the cathode material, the solvents, the conducting salts used for the production of the electrolyte and electrolyte additives.
Conclusions and Perspective

The anode/electrolyte interphase (SEI) plays a key role in alkali-metal, alkali-metal ion and alkaline-earth batteries. It was found that ionic migration through the SEI is the rate-determining step for many systems. In this case, the Tafel slope (b) is a linear function of the thickness of the SEI. Under OCV conditions, the increase in the thickness of the SEI follows a parabolic law with respect to the time of storage. The study of SEI properties in future systems including lithium/sulfur, lithium/air, sodium, sodium-ion and calcium batteries is in its early stage. A combination of a variety of analytical tools, including dilatometry, STM, AFM, XPS, EDS, SEM, XRD, QCMB, FTIR, NMR, EPR, TOF SIMS, Raman spectroscopy, AC-impedance measurements and DSC is used in order to gain a comprehensive characterization of the processes occurring at the anode/electrolyte interphase.

Molecular dynamics (MD) and density-functional theory (DFT) simulation methods have been used to gain an understanding of the relationship between the SEI layer and the electrolyte. The use of Abinitio molecular dynamics (AIMD) has extended MD capability by combining the Schrodinger wave equation with Newton's equations.

The SEI is formed by parallel and competing reduction reactions and thus its composition depends on \( \mathrm{i}_0 \), \( \eta \) and the concentrations of all the electroactive materials in the electrolyte. Close to the electrode side, the SEI consists of fully reduced (thermodynamically stable) anions such as \( \mathrm{F}^{-} \), \( \mathrm{O}^{2-} \), \( \mathrm{S}^{2-} \) and other elements like As, B, C (or their lithiated compounds). The outer part of the SEI (near the solution) consists of partially reduced materials like polyolefins, polyTHF, \( \mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3 \), \( \mathrm{LiRCO}_3 \), R-O-Li, LiOH, and LiF, LiCl, \( \mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{O} \), etc. TOF SIMS measurements provide direct evidence for the existence of polymers in the basal SEI.

The true lateral size of the micro domains in HOPG and graphite electrodes, is about or less than 1 micron. Since the SEI consists of a mosaic of heteropolymicrophases, the contribution of grain boundaries must be considered. The equivalent circuit of the SEI is extremely complex and should be represented by a very large number of series and parallel distributions of RC elements, reflecting bulk ionic conductivity and grain-boundary phenomena, aside from the Warburg element.

For carbon anodes, the exchange-current density of the reduction reactions also depends on the surface properties of the electrode (content of impurities, surface chemistry and surface morphology). The surface chemistry and morphology of the SEI vary with the ratio of basal-plane to edge-plane areas of carbons. We have suggested that the carbon matrix has a more marked effect on the composition and thickness of the SEI than does the nature of the electrolyte. Mild oxidation of graphite was found to improve anode performance. Improvement was attributed to the formation of SEI chemically bonded (CB-SEI) to the surface carboxylic and oxide groups at the zig-zag and armchair faces, better wetting by the electrolyte and to accommodation of extra lithium at the zig-zag, armchair and other edge sites and nano voids. Graphite-surface modification by silylation and the creation of the core-shell-structured carbon composite look promising for the enhancement of electrochemical performance. In lithium-ion batteries, with carbonaceous anodes, \(\mathrm{Q_{IR}}\) can be lowered by decreasing the true surface area of the carbon, using pure carbon and electrolyte, applying an optimal charge protocol at the first charge (system-dependent) and by using proper electrolyte component combinations and SEI precursors. In some systems, increasing charging rates for fast SEI

formation, results in incomplete, non-uniform, electrochemically unstable layers or deposits, thereby lowering cell efficiency, durability and safety. Similarly, insufficient electrolyte wetting leads to a low-quality SEI layer, inactive surface area, and/or premature degradation of cell performance. EIS measurements of a cycled lithium/sulfur cell reveal that, in some cases, \(\mathrm{R_{SEI}}\) almost does not change during the first 200 cycles. Capacity fading results from the increase of both \(\mathrm{R_{SEI}}\) and \(\mathrm{R_{CT}}\) and the decrease in diffusion coefficient (D) during prolonged cycling.[162] D decreases as a result of several processes, including partial blocking of the cathode surface, precipitation of electrolyte reduction products on the anode (as a secondary SEI) and into the separator. In this system, reducing electrolyte reduction by the anode is essential for achieving a long cycle life.

An understanding of SEI-related phenomena is crucial for the development of safer and better SEI batteries. Today we have a good understanding of SEI-formation processes, kinetics of SEI electrodes and SEI growth in common systems, mainly lithium-ion and some lithium-metal batteries. However, in general, actual SEIs do not yet seem to have optimal properties; they continue to grow over repeated charge/discharge cycles and over long storage and dissolve at elevated temperatures creating dangerous situations and for silicon anodes they do not provide the required durability. Intensive research is currently ongoing to improve SEI properties and to develop an artificial SEI. Progress is slowly being made in increasing the durability of lithium ion and lithium sulfur batteries and silicon based anodes.
